children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29B.1	The Department of Operational Support is the operational arm of the Secretariat and the client facing interface for operational support matters in the Secretariat’s management structure, which exists to support the objective of effective mandate delivery, in partnership with Secretariat entities and other clients. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 72/266 B and 73/281. The Department delivers rapid, effective, efficient, responsible and, where required, customized enabling solutions that help partners to meet their mandates, including in the most challenging operating environments.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29B.2	In 2027, the Department will continue to support effective mandate implementation through the delivery of operational support. In executing its programme, the Department will partner with client entities to identify needs and jointly develop solutions, including in the areas of end-to-end supply chain management, support for uniformed personnel, human resources, a broad range of training services, business process improvement, medical and occupational health and safety, and information and communications technology services delivered through the Office of Information and Communications Technology. In exceptional circumstances, the Department will exercise delegated authority on behalf of client entities across the Secretariat. The Department will lead the implementation of the common administrative platform at Headquarters in New York, providing support to some 50 entities and their personnel in New York and, to various extents, their personnel located outside New York. The Department will also provide advisory support to guide the implementation of the common administrative platform in Bangkok and the establishment of additional common administrative platforms in Addis Ababa, Beirut, Geneva, Nairobi and Vienna.

29B.3	The Department will seek to strike an appropriate balance between direct service delivery and advisory support, adapting its approach to diverse client requirements and promoting a positive client experience. It will adapt its planning to anticipate and respond to changes in the operating environment, fostering a culture that is agile, responsive and forward-looking. The Department will also leverage technology, automation, innovation and high-quality data to simplify and streamline processes. In addition, it will assist relevant Secretariat actors in integrating positive legacy considerations into the planning and management of United Nations operations, including facilities, infrastructure and services, to maximize potential secondary benefits for host communities.

29B.4	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Department will strengthen collaboration with Member States and regional organizations, including through capacity-building initiatives, knowledge-sharing and functional arrangements across areas of operational support, with a view to achieving the scale and effectiveness required to address complex operational challenges.

29B.5	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will further advance its operating model for the provision of a comprehensive range of services within and beyond the United Nations Secretariat, guided by comparative advantage. In doing so, it will lead and coordinate a network of service providers, where the Department focuses its own efforts on enhancing service delivery and overall effectiveness.

29B.6	With regard to the external factors, the Department considered applicable risks identified in its risk register, and, accordingly, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	There is adequate support from relevant stakeholders to enable the delivery of effective operational support solutions in challenging operating environments;
(b)	Requirements for crisis response and other special situations will not exceed the Department’s capacity to respond.

29B.7	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. For example, activities will include camp design methodologies, improvements in the area of women’s health, staffing initiatives such as the senior women talent pipeline, and advice to field entities, upon request, to enable them to meet targets related to gender parity. In order to encourage a more equitable approach to the Organization’s supply chain, the Department will continue to strengthen outreach and training for vendors, including women-owned businesses.

29B.8	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department will support efforts to improve accessibility for persons with disabilities to the United Nations premises, to enable their full, meaningful and effective participation in the work of the United Nations, as outlined in result 3 under subprogramme 4. In addition, the Department will enhance accessibility for persons with disabilities to its training materials developed under subprogramme 1 component 2, and will further integrate disability inclusion across its global procurement activities, including through targeted outreach to disability-inclusive vendors under subprogramme 2, component 1.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

29B.9	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, as outlined under paragraph 29B.2, the Department is leading the development and implementation of the common administrative platform in New York and supporting the implementation and/or establishment of common administrative platforms in other locations. These changes have also affected the targets for result 2 under subprogramme 3. Furthermore, payroll operations have been centralized into one team located in three global centres hosted at Headquarters in New York, the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, and the United Nations Office at Nairobi and reporting to the Department, as reflected in the updated strategy of subprogramme 4. Serving approximately 55,000 personnel, this consolidation of payroll functions will enhance internal controls, standardize processes and provide greater consistency and accountability in payroll services.

29B.10	Required adjustments to the Department’s programme of work have also been implemented. Some changes enable greater efficiency and resilience, while others reflect efforts to maintain core functions. For example, the number of operational and language learning initiatives will not be further expanded, as reflected under result 2 under subprogramme 1, component 2. In the area of healthcare management, the implementation of healthcare quality and patient safety standards will follow a risk-based approach, with an initial emphasis on level 2+ hospital facilities where clinical complexity and patient safety risk are highest, as reflected in the updated strategy of subprogramme 1, component 3. The completion of facilities and commercial services requests will be scaled down, as reflected in the table of deliverables of subprogramme 4.

29B.11	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals to further consolidate operational support services.

children.text: Evaluation activities
29B.14	An evaluation of the Department’s standing surge capacity mechanism, completed by the Department in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

29B.15	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Department will implement recommendations to improve the efficiency of the standing surge capacity mechanism and explore opportunities to enhance the mechanism so that it goes beyond serving solely as a technical tool for crisis management, evolving into a more strategic approach that can be expanded across the United Nations common system.
29B.16	An evaluation of the walking blood bank project, to be conducted by the Department, is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Support operations
Component 1 – Human resources support – Objective

29B.17	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to strengthen the human resources capacity of all Secretariat entities, enable improved, simplified human resources processes and offer the Organization talented and diverse staffing pools.
Strategy
29B.18	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Support Secretariat entities in the exercise of delegated authority in the area of human resources, including through:

(i)	Specialized advisory support and guidance to human resources business partners across the Secretariat, including on dispute prevention and resolution, affiliated-personnel capacities and other entity-specific operational human resources needs;

(ii)	Active engagement and regular analysis of support requests, with a view to communicating required policy changes to the Department of Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance on the basis of client needs;

(iii)	Simplification and digitalization of human resources processes, tools and procedures, such as recruitment, rostering, testing and examination services, affiliated-personnel capacities and workforce planning, in cooperation with the Office of Human Resources and the Office of Information and Communications Technology;
(b)	Offer high-quality and diverse pools of candidates by managing global Secretariat rosters across all job families;
(c)	Administer the young professionals programme.
29B.19	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Secretariat entities accountably, effectively and efficiently exercising their delegated human resources authority in support of their mandates;
(b)	Increased efficiencies and measurable reductions in delays and redundancies in human resources processes;
(c)	Strengthened capacity for the timely identification and recruitment of candidates and talent that are the best fit.
29B.31	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Design, develop and deliver a diverse portfolio of training and capacity-building services tailored to operational needs in collaboration with Secretariat entities; create and execute comprehensive training programmes across key operational areas, such as resources stewardship, data-related capacities and other support functions, including Umoja; and emphasize the integration of e-learning platforms and apply change management strategies to facilitate effective learning outcomes and seamless adaptation;
(b)	Strengthen knowledge management and innovative information-sharing to boost operational effectiveness through standardized, accessible guidance and process improvement initiatives;
(c)	Reinforce and refocus the language and communications training and resources to further advance linguistic diversity in the context of multilingualism.
29B.32	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Stronger organizational governance by senior leadership teams, as well as better trained and empowered staff to support operational decision-making, resource management and improved operational processes;

(b)	Knowledge management and learning management platforms that reach a broad spectrum of practitioners across the Secretariat, resulting in strengthened awareness and competence with regard to resource stewardship and other operational responsibilities;
(c)	Greater linguistic capability and diversity and improved communication capacities across the Secretariat in support of multilingualism.
29B.44	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Ensure standardized credentialing for United Nations healthcare personnel, with priority given to high-risk and high-volume missions, so that healthcare personnel with appropriate qualifications and skills are deployed to field duty stations;

(b)	Implement healthcare quality and patient safety standards at all United Nations healthcare facilities at level 1+ and above, through selective facility assessments and the review of relevant quality and patient safety data;
(c)	Develop evidence-based health promotion and disease prevention guidance for implementation by United Nations medical services globally;
(d)	Implement key elements of workplace risk management for Secretariat entities, including incident reporting and oversight through policy, standards and guidance;

(e)	Maintain essential business processes for medical entitlements and medical operations, including the use of the electronic medical record system and the integration of data related to absence due to sickness, medical evacuations and service-incurred compensation;
(f)	Improve access of personnel of peace operations to health services, including women’s and mental health services, in line with paragraph 42 (d) of the Pact for the Future.
29B.45	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A reduction in adverse events and preventable harm through strengthened medical governance and patient safety oversight;
(b)	Continued identification and mitigation of priority workplace and health-related risks;
(c)	Sustained compliance with United Nations healthcare quality and patient safety standards in field and referral facilities.
Programme performance in 2025
Secretariat entities receive comprehensive operational human resources support and guidance for restructuring and downsizing

29B.20	In the context of mission transitions, evolving operational priorities and resource constraints, several Secretariat entities have undertaken restructuring, downsizing and contingency planning measures. These developments have occurred alongside broader organizational reforms under the UN80 Initiative. The component provided extensive support to Secretariat entities with policy and operational guidance, including briefings, guidance materials and toolkits on the downsizing policy. This support was essential to maintain business continuity and mitigate the impact on staff and personnel during periods of significant change. The component also supported United Nations peace operations in implementing contingency planning measures and other entities undergoing downsizing related to reductions in extrabudgetary funding.

children.text: United Nations staff and personnel, Member States, job candidates and hiring managers have access to a centralized hub for language and communications skills

29B.33	In 2025, the component launched the first centralized digital platform for language and communications learning in the six official languages of the United Nations. The United Nations languages website established a standardized and structured language and communications learning ecosystem with diverse assets that span language development courses, high-impact communication workshops, self-assessment tools and customized training solutions in the six official languages, expanding the multilingual skills of the workforce at the Organization. The new platform functions as a resource hub for accessible, inclusive and innovative programming aligned with the United Nations Language Framework and illustrative multimedia samples of all levels in the six official languages. Sustained outreach during enrolment periods alongside the component’s Secretariat-wide initiatives, including the flagship United Nations “Skills Fest”, significantly furthered the visibility of and engagement with the new platform. Beneficiaries across the world, including United Nations staff, Member States, job candidates and hiring managers, gained a deeper familiarity of opportunities to enhance their language and communication skills, support career mobility and enhance job function and professional development.
29B.34	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29B.I).

Performance measure: number of views and number of active users of the United Nations languages website
Improved preparedness to prevent and mitigate communicable disease risks affecting United Nations staff and personnel

29B.46	Communicable disease outbreaks continue to pose a significant risk to the health of United Nations staff and personnel and the continuity of operations, in particular in duty stations with limited health infrastructure or heightened exposure risks. The component has strengthened organizational preparedness by developing or updating comprehensive communicable disease risk mitigation plans, providing duty stations and staff with clear, practical guidance to prevent, detect and mitigate the risk of communicable disease outbreaks. These plans consolidate risk assessment prevention measures and response protocols, supporting consistent and timely action across diverse operating environments.
29B.47	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29B.V).

children.text: Result 3: enhanced recruitment of gratis personnel – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.26	The recruitment and nomination process for gratis personnel (gratis personnel type II and experts on non-reimbursable loan) relies on paper-based workflows, which create inefficiencies and lack transparency. Individual entities reach out to Member States separately by email, resulting in fragmented communication, limited visibility of opportunities and delays in processing nominations and resumes. The component has undertaken measures to streamline and modernize the process. Efforts have focused on identifying bottlenecks in the existing system and assessing digital solutions to strengthen coordination and transparency.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.27	The lesson for the component was the need to establish a centralized and efficient system to prevent delays, increase the visibility of opportunities and reduce administrative burden. In applying the lesson, the component has developed an automated online workflow in Inspira that consolidates all opportunities in a dedicated portal. This will enable entities to publish vacancies centrally, allow Member States to submit nominations online and facilitate candidates’ direct application to vacancies. These enhancements will improve efficiency, accountability and equitable access to opportunities. The automated online workflow also allows managers to utilize existing screening tools in Inspira to review submitted applications in a more consistent, transparent and fair manner. The component will roll out the new workflow and provide training for entities and Member States’ representatives.
29B.28	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29B.5).

Result 3: improved availability and accessibility of administrative and operational guidance – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.39	Effective access to timely, accurate and harmonized administrative and operational guidance is essential for staff to perform their functions efficiently, in particular in an environment characterized by increased operational complexity, geographically dispersed duty stations and continuing resource constraints. The component has established the Knowledge Gateway as a centralized digital platform providing validated administrative and operational guidance and best practices across more than 148 thematic areas. The platform has proved to be highly effective, attracting more than 4 million views and supporting staff across the Secretariat in navigating administrative and operational requirements. Building on this strong foundation, the component will further enhance the Knowledge Gateway by leveraging digital technology to improve the discoverability, accessibility and usability of authoritative guidance.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.40	The lesson for the component was that even highly utilized digital platforms required continuous improvement. Analysis of usage patterns and user feedback highlighted opportunities to further strengthen search and navigation capabilities to ensure that staff could locate relevant guidance as efficiently as possible at the point of need. In applying this lesson, the component will enhance the existing Knowledge Gateway through the introduction of an artificial intelligence-enabled chat search functionality, enabling more intuitive, query-based access to harmonized administrative and operational information, while continuing to rely on validated and authoritative sources.
29B.41	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29B.IV).
Figure 29B.IV

Result 3: enhanced access to digital mental health support for peacekeeping personnel – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.52	Peacekeeping personnel face heightened psychological stress arising from operational exposure, isolation and complex security environments. The component develops and deploys digital mental health tools and resources designed to be accessible, scalable and suitable for diverse field settings. Building on work undertaken in prior years, the component has expanded the availability of digital mental health support products for both uniformed and civilian peacekeeping personnel, facilitating early access to guidance, self-support tools and referral pathways.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.53	The lesson for the component was that digital delivery mechanisms enabled the broader, more timely and cost-effective reach of mental health support in peace operations, in particular where in-person services were limited. In applying this lesson, the component will prioritize the use of standardized digital means for providing mental health support rather than expanding resource-intensive in-person models and will focus on increasing the awareness and accessibility of existing tools.

29B.19	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Secretariat entities accountably, effectively and efficiently exercising their delegated human resources authority in support of their mandates;
(b)	Increased efficiencies and measurable reductions in delays and redundancies in human resources processes;
(c)	Strengthened capacity for the timely identification and recruitment of candidates and talent that are the best fit.
29B.31	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Design, develop and deliver a diverse portfolio of training and capacity-building services tailored to operational needs in collaboration with Secretariat entities; create and execute comprehensive training programmes across key operational areas, such as resources stewardship, data-related capacities and other support functions, including Umoja; and emphasize the integration of e-learning platforms and apply change management strategies to facilitate effective learning outcomes and seamless adaptation;
(b)	Strengthen knowledge management and innovative information-sharing to boost operational effectiveness through standardized, accessible guidance and process improvement initiatives;
(c)	Reinforce and refocus the language and communications training and resources to further advance linguistic diversity in the context of multilingualism.
29B.32	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Stronger organizational governance by senior leadership teams, as well as better trained and empowered staff to support operational decision-making, resource management and improved operational processes;

(b)	Knowledge management and learning management platforms that reach a broad spectrum of practitioners across the Secretariat, resulting in strengthened awareness and competence with regard to resource stewardship and other operational responsibilities;
(c)	Greater linguistic capability and diversity and improved communication capacities across the Secretariat in support of multilingualism.
29B.44	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Ensure standardized credentialing for United Nations healthcare personnel, with priority given to high-risk and high-volume missions, so that healthcare personnel with appropriate qualifications and skills are deployed to field duty stations;

(b)	Implement healthcare quality and patient safety standards at all United Nations healthcare facilities at level 1+ and above, through selective facility assessments and the review of relevant quality and patient safety data;
(c)	Develop evidence-based health promotion and disease prevention guidance for implementation by United Nations medical services globally;
(d)	Implement key elements of workplace risk management for Secretariat entities, including incident reporting and oversight through policy, standards and guidance;

(e)	Maintain essential business processes for medical entitlements and medical operations, including the use of the electronic medical record system and the integration of data related to absence due to sickness, medical evacuations and service-incurred compensation;
(f)	Improve access of personnel of peace operations to health services, including women’s and mental health services, in line with paragraph 42 (d) of the Pact for the Future.
29B.45	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A reduction in adverse events and preventable harm through strengthened medical governance and patient safety oversight;
(b)	Continued identification and mitigation of priority workplace and health-related risks;
(c)	Sustained compliance with United Nations healthcare quality and patient safety standards in field and referral facilities.

children.text: United Nations staff and personnel, Member States, job candidates and hiring managers have access to a centralized hub for language and communications skills

29B.33	In 2025, the component launched the first centralized digital platform for language and communications learning in the six official languages of the United Nations. The United Nations languages website established a standardized and structured language and communications learning ecosystem with diverse assets that span language development courses, high-impact communication workshops, self-assessment tools and customized training solutions in the six official languages, expanding the multilingual skills of the workforce at the Organization. The new platform functions as a resource hub for accessible, inclusive and innovative programming aligned with the United Nations Language Framework and illustrative multimedia samples of all levels in the six official languages. Sustained outreach during enrolment periods alongside the component’s Secretariat-wide initiatives, including the flagship United Nations “Skills Fest”, significantly furthered the visibility of and engagement with the new platform. Beneficiaries across the world, including United Nations staff, Member States, job candidates and hiring managers, gained a deeper familiarity of opportunities to enhance their language and communication skills, support career mobility and enhance job function and professional development.

children.text: Result 3: enhanced recruitment of gratis personnel – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.26	The recruitment and nomination process for gratis personnel (gratis personnel type II and experts on non-reimbursable loan) relies on paper-based workflows, which create inefficiencies and lack transparency. Individual entities reach out to Member States separately by email, resulting in fragmented communication, limited visibility of opportunities and delays in processing nominations and resumes. The component has undertaken measures to streamline and modernize the process. Efforts have focused on identifying bottlenecks in the existing system and assessing digital solutions to strengthen coordination and transparency.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.27	The lesson for the component was the need to establish a centralized and efficient system to prevent delays, increase the visibility of opportunities and reduce administrative burden. In applying the lesson, the component has developed an automated online workflow in Inspira that consolidates all opportunities in a dedicated portal. This will enable entities to publish vacancies centrally, allow Member States to submit nominations online and facilitate candidates’ direct application to vacancies. These enhancements will improve efficiency, accountability and equitable access to opportunities. The automated online workflow also allows managers to utilize existing screening tools in Inspira to review submitted applications in a more consistent, transparent and fair manner. The component will roll out the new workflow and provide training for entities and Member States’ representatives.

Result 3: improved availability and accessibility of administrative and operational guidance – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.39	Effective access to timely, accurate and harmonized administrative and operational guidance is essential for staff to perform their functions efficiently, in particular in an environment characterized by increased operational complexity, geographically dispersed duty stations and continuing resource constraints. The component has established the Knowledge Gateway as a centralized digital platform providing validated administrative and operational guidance and best practices across more than 148 thematic areas. The platform has proved to be highly effective, attracting more than 4 million views and supporting staff across the Secretariat in navigating administrative and operational requirements. Building on this strong foundation, the component will further enhance the Knowledge Gateway by leveraging digital technology to improve the discoverability, accessibility and usability of authoritative guidance.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.40	The lesson for the component was that even highly utilized digital platforms required continuous improvement. Analysis of usage patterns and user feedback highlighted opportunities to further strengthen search and navigation capabilities to ensure that staff could locate relevant guidance as efficiently as possible at the point of need. In applying this lesson, the component will enhance the existing Knowledge Gateway through the introduction of an artificial intelligence-enabled chat search functionality, enabling more intuitive, query-based access to harmonized administrative and operational information, while continuing to rely on validated and authoritative sources.

Result 3: enhanced access to digital mental health support for peacekeeping personnel – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.52	Peacekeeping personnel face heightened psychological stress arising from operational exposure, isolation and complex security environments. The component develops and deploys digital mental health tools and resources designed to be accessible, scalable and suitable for diverse field settings. Building on work undertaken in prior years, the component has expanded the availability of digital mental health support products for both uniformed and civilian peacekeeping personnel, facilitating early access to guidance, self-support tools and referral pathways.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.53	The lesson for the component was that digital delivery mechanisms enabled the broader, more timely and cost-effective reach of mental health support in peace operations, in particular where in-person services were limited. In applying this lesson, the component will prioritize the use of standardized digital means for providing mental health support rather than expanding resource-intensive in-person models and will focus on increasing the awareness and accessibility of existing tools.

Result 3: improved availability and accessibility of administrative and operational guidance – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.39	Effective access to timely, accurate and harmonized administrative and operational guidance is essential for staff to perform their functions efficiently, in particular in an environment characterized by increased operational complexity, geographically dispersed duty stations and continuing resource constraints. The component has established the Knowledge Gateway as a centralized digital platform providing validated administrative and operational guidance and best practices across more than 148 thematic areas. The platform has proved to be highly effective, attracting more than 4 million views and supporting staff across the Secretariat in navigating administrative and operational requirements. Building on this strong foundation, the component will further enhance the Knowledge Gateway by leveraging digital technology to improve the discoverability, accessibility and usability of authoritative guidance.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.40	The lesson for the component was that even highly utilized digital platforms required continuous improvement. Analysis of usage patterns and user feedback highlighted opportunities to further strengthen search and navigation capabilities to ensure that staff could locate relevant guidance as efficiently as possible at the point of need. In applying this lesson, the component will enhance the existing Knowledge Gateway through the introduction of an artificial intelligence-enabled chat search functionality, enabling more intuitive, query-based access to harmonized administrative and operational information, while continuing to rely on validated and authoritative sources.

Result 3: enhanced access to digital mental health support for peacekeeping personnel – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.52	Peacekeeping personnel face heightened psychological stress arising from operational exposure, isolation and complex security environments. The component develops and deploys digital mental health tools and resources designed to be accessible, scalable and suitable for diverse field settings. Building on work undertaken in prior years, the component has expanded the availability of digital mental health support products for both uniformed and civilian peacekeeping personnel, facilitating early access to guidance, self-support tools and referral pathways.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.53	The lesson for the component was that digital delivery mechanisms enabled the broader, more timely and cost-effective reach of mental health support in peace operations, in particular where in-person services were limited. In applying this lesson, the component will prioritize the use of standardized digital means for providing mental health support rather than expanding resource-intensive in-person models and will focus on increasing the awareness and accessibility of existing tools.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Supply chain management
Component 1 – Integrated supply chain management – Objective

29B.56	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to ensure the availability of the right goods and services at the right place, the right time and the right costs for effective and efficient mandate implementation by United Nations Secretariat client entities.
Strategy
29B.57	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Improve alignment of the Secretariat’s end-to-end supply chain with the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and operationalize 24-month rolling supply chain planning, for continuous monitoring and update of supply chain plans to meet operational requirements and priorities;

(b)	Identify and ensure the availability of appropriate sourcing options and solutions, such as global and regional systems contracts, local procurement, letters of assist and memorandums of understanding, or the leveraging of existing stock;

(c)	Continue outreach efforts to facilitate diversity and proportionate participation in business opportunities with the United Nations, including through targeted outreach to Member States and the enhanced use of business seminars, in particular for women-owned and disability-inclusive businesses and vendors from developing countries and countries with economies in transition;
(d)	Maintain the list of strategic goods and services sourced through Headquarters to improve risk management in procurement, economies of scale and standardization;
(e)	Support all entities with the timely and effective sourcing and delivery of goods and services, including the fulfilment of unforeseen requirements.
29B.58	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The availability of the right goods and services at the right time, while taking into consideration their full cost of ownership;
(b)	Client entities gaining access to appropriate, effective and efficient sourcing and delivery solutions to implement their mandates;
(c)	A more diversified vendor roster for more inclusive and effective international competition.
29B.70	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Serve as the single point of contact for troop- and police-contributing countries on all administrative and logistical issues related to force generation, memorandums of understanding, contingent-owned equipment and reimbursement;

(b)	Support the periodic review by the General Assembly of the reimbursement framework associated with uniformed capabilities deployed in formed units, support the negotiation and finalization of memorandums of understanding with troop- and police-contributing countries, expeditiously process applicable reimbursements in line with the decisions of the Assembly and facilitate the strategic integration of relevant performance data related to deployed contingent-owned equipment;
(c)	Provide a streamlined reimbursement process involving an enhanced memorandum of understanding and payment processes;
(d)	Provide strategic and operational support and foster enhanced partnerships with troop- and police-contributing countries.
29B.71	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Servicing of the General Assembly during the periodic review of the reimbursement framework for formed units and the implementation of all derivative guidance;

(b)	Streamlined and timely payments to troop- and police-contributing countries for uniformed personnel and contingent-owned equipment deployed in formed units, letter-of-assist reimbursements, death and disability claims and ad hoc claims;
(c)	Increased transparency and accountability of the reimbursement process and the real-time availability of data to Member States, Headquarters and field missions.
Programme performance in 2025
Safe and timely repatriation of uniformed personnel in line with mission contingency plans

29B.59	The liquidity crisis faced by the Organization has entailed significant challenges for peacekeeping operations. In 2025, the Secretary-General requested that all peacekeeping missions funded under the peacekeeping budget implement their contingency plans for a 15 per cent reduction in expenditure. This has necessitated a reduction in the size of military and police units in peacekeeping operations and the repatriation of some uniformed personnel. The component worked closely with all field missions to ensure the safe and timely repatriation of uniformed personnel. This effort was far more complex than a routine repatriation because it required urgent, large‑scale, contingency‑driven movements under severe time, financial, logistical and diplomatic pressures that far exceeded normal rotational planning. The approach emphasized minimizing the impact on mandate delivery and personnel while maintaining strategic engagement with stakeholders. As a result of the effort, in close coordination with field missions 10,785 uniformed personnel were repatriated between 8 October and 31 December 2025, representing 74.1 per cent of the total number of uniformed personnel to be repatriated as part of the mission contingency plans.

children.text: Result 3: integrated supply chain solutions for humanitarian and complex environments – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.65	As part of the humanitarian reset, the component has been supporting a system-wide initiative to reprioritize resources and streamline operations in response to reduced humanitarian funding, with a view to enabling the United Nations to “deliver as one” through more efficient and integrated supply chain solutions. In collaboration with the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, the United Nations Children’s Fund and the World Food Programme, the component developed an integrated approach to delivering supply chain solutions in humanitarian and complex operational environments.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.66	The lesson for the component was that fragmented, entity-specific arrangements limited the ability of the United Nations system to deliver in a coherent manner, reduce the efficiency and timeliness of support and increase operational burdens on field teams. In applying the lesson, the component will operationalize an integrated approach focused on three areas: (a) coordinated procurement of key items through demand aggregation, technical collaboration, joint market engagement and strategic contracting; (b) optimizing global freight by pooling access to optimized global logistics services and leveraging pre-negotiated contracts, established supplier networks and shared infrastructure; and (c) harmonized in-country logistics, including import processing, customs clearance, warehousing, inventory management and humanitarian fleet operations. This approach will improve the speed and reliability of international transport and upstream logistics, reduce burdens on field teams and enable the more efficient delivery of assistance. The integrated service offer will be provided as an opt-in model, with services provided on a transparent, full cost-recovery basis, enabling entities to reduce or phase out duplicative capacities, as appropriate, while respecting mandates.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened management of contingent-owned ammunition – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.78	Effective ammunition management is critical to ensure operational readiness, accountability and safety, while minimizing risks of diversion, loss or accidental incidents. Managing ammunition inventory can help to ensure the accurate tracking, transparency and life-cycle monitoring of ammunition, enabling informed decision-making, timely reporting and oversight across field missions. The component manages the Uniformed Capabilities Management System, a platform that streamlines and enables end-to-end planning for uniformed capabilities management for troop- and police-contributing countries.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.79	The lesson for the component was that the absence of a centralized repository for ammunition limited the ability of field missions and Headquarters to monitor ammunition status and plan for timely disposals and replenishments. In applying the lesson, the component developed, in close coordination with the Department of Peace Operations, field missions and the Office of Information and Communications Technology, a new module in the Uniformed Capabilities Management System to record, monitor and manage contingent-owned ammunition. This ammunition management module will standardize the ammunition inventory across all missions and enable field missions to generate inspections and record all transactions throughout the life cycle of the contingent-owned ammunition. This module will be further enriched to include performance analyses and data reporting, as well as additional functionalities related to ammunition claims and reimbursements.

Component 2 – Uniformed capabilities support – Objective
29B.69	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to ensure the efficient and effective functioning of the peace and security pillar of the United Nations.
Strategy
29B.57	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Improve alignment of the Secretariat’s end-to-end supply chain with the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and operationalize 24-month rolling supply chain planning, for continuous monitoring and update of supply chain plans to meet operational requirements and priorities;

(b)	Identify and ensure the availability of appropriate sourcing options and solutions, such as global and regional systems contracts, local procurement, letters of assist and memorandums of understanding, or the leveraging of existing stock;

(c)	Continue outreach efforts to facilitate diversity and proportionate participation in business opportunities with the United Nations, including through targeted outreach to Member States and the enhanced use of business seminars, in particular for women-owned and disability-inclusive businesses and vendors from developing countries and countries with economies in transition;
(d)	Maintain the list of strategic goods and services sourced through Headquarters to improve risk management in procurement, economies of scale and standardization;
(e)	Support all entities with the timely and effective sourcing and delivery of goods and services, including the fulfilment of unforeseen requirements.
29B.58	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The availability of the right goods and services at the right time, while taking into consideration their full cost of ownership;
(b)	Client entities gaining access to appropriate, effective and efficient sourcing and delivery solutions to implement their mandates;
(c)	A more diversified vendor roster for more inclusive and effective international competition.
29B.70	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Serve as the single point of contact for troop- and police-contributing countries on all administrative and logistical issues related to force generation, memorandums of understanding, contingent-owned equipment and reimbursement;

(b)	Support the periodic review by the General Assembly of the reimbursement framework associated with uniformed capabilities deployed in formed units, support the negotiation and finalization of memorandums of understanding with troop- and police-contributing countries, expeditiously process applicable reimbursements in line with the decisions of the Assembly and facilitate the strategic integration of relevant performance data related to deployed contingent-owned equipment;
(c)	Provide a streamlined reimbursement process involving an enhanced memorandum of understanding and payment processes;
(d)	Provide strategic and operational support and foster enhanced partnerships with troop- and police-contributing countries.
29B.71	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Servicing of the General Assembly during the periodic review of the reimbursement framework for formed units and the implementation of all derivative guidance;

(b)	Streamlined and timely payments to troop- and police-contributing countries for uniformed personnel and contingent-owned equipment deployed in formed units, letter-of-assist reimbursements, death and disability claims and ad hoc claims;
(c)	Increased transparency and accountability of the reimbursement process and the real-time availability of data to Member States, Headquarters and field missions.
Programme performance in 2025
Safe and timely repatriation of uniformed personnel in line with mission contingency plans

29B.59	The liquidity crisis faced by the Organization has entailed significant challenges for peacekeeping operations. In 2025, the Secretary-General requested that all peacekeeping missions funded under the peacekeeping budget implement their contingency plans for a 15 per cent reduction in expenditure. This has necessitated a reduction in the size of military and police units in peacekeeping operations and the repatriation of some uniformed personnel. The component worked closely with all field missions to ensure the safe and timely repatriation of uniformed personnel. This effort was far more complex than a routine repatriation because it required urgent, large‑scale, contingency‑driven movements under severe time, financial, logistical and diplomatic pressures that far exceeded normal rotational planning. The approach emphasized minimizing the impact on mandate delivery and personnel while maintaining strategic engagement with stakeholders. As a result of the effort, in close coordination with field missions 10,785 uniformed personnel were repatriated between 8 October and 31 December 2025, representing 74.1 per cent of the total number of uniformed personnel to be repatriated as part of the mission contingency plans.

children.text: Result 3: integrated supply chain solutions for humanitarian and complex environments – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.65	As part of the humanitarian reset, the component has been supporting a system-wide initiative to reprioritize resources and streamline operations in response to reduced humanitarian funding, with a view to enabling the United Nations to “deliver as one” through more efficient and integrated supply chain solutions. In collaboration with the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, the United Nations Children’s Fund and the World Food Programme, the component developed an integrated approach to delivering supply chain solutions in humanitarian and complex operational environments.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.66	The lesson for the component was that fragmented, entity-specific arrangements limited the ability of the United Nations system to deliver in a coherent manner, reduce the efficiency and timeliness of support and increase operational burdens on field teams. In applying the lesson, the component will operationalize an integrated approach focused on three areas: (a) coordinated procurement of key items through demand aggregation, technical collaboration, joint market engagement and strategic contracting; (b) optimizing global freight by pooling access to optimized global logistics services and leveraging pre-negotiated contracts, established supplier networks and shared infrastructure; and (c) harmonized in-country logistics, including import processing, customs clearance, warehousing, inventory management and humanitarian fleet operations. This approach will improve the speed and reliability of international transport and upstream logistics, reduce burdens on field teams and enable the more efficient delivery of assistance. The integrated service offer will be provided as an opt-in model, with services provided on a transparent, full cost-recovery basis, enabling entities to reduce or phase out duplicative capacities, as appropriate, while respecting mandates.

This functionality is to be implemented in 2026. children.text: Result 3: strengthened management of contingent-owned ammunition – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.78	Effective ammunition management is critical to ensure operational readiness, accountability and safety, while minimizing risks of diversion, loss or accidental incidents. Managing ammunition inventory can help to ensure the accurate tracking, transparency and life-cycle monitoring of ammunition, enabling informed decision-making, timely reporting and oversight across field missions. The component manages the Uniformed Capabilities Management System, a platform that streamlines and enables end-to-end planning for uniformed capabilities management for troop- and police-contributing countries.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.79	The lesson for the component was that the absence of a centralized repository for ammunition limited the ability of field missions and Headquarters to monitor ammunition status and plan for timely disposals and replenishments. In applying the lesson, the component developed, in close coordination with the Department of Peace Operations, field missions and the Office of Information and Communications Technology, a new module in the Uniformed Capabilities Management System to record, monitor and manage contingent-owned ammunition. This ammunition management module will standardize the ammunition inventory across all missions and enable field missions to generate inspections and record all transactions throughout the life cycle of the contingent-owned ammunition. This module will be further enriched to include performance analyses and data reporting, as well as additional functionalities related to ammunition claims and reimbursements.

Subprogramme 3 – Special activities
Objective
29B.82	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure effective and efficient operational support responses by the Secretariat, including for unique and escalated requirements.
Strategy
29B.83	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Lead and coordinate the Secretariat’s operational support response to emerging situations and other unique and surge-level requirements, including through planning support and monitoring, shared situational awareness, the provision of surge and specialized capacities and the facilitation of coordinated efforts;

(b)	Lead efforts to redesign and strengthen the capacities, efficiency and responsiveness of the Secretariat’s operational support architecture, including by working with Secretariat and United Nations system entities on the establishment and implementation of the common administrative platforms, global payroll services and the United Nations system unified services road map, as well as by providing direct support for the establishment and implementation of United Nations system partnerships for Secretariat entities;

(c)	Coordinate and maintain bilateral and other multiparty relationships with key non-Secretariat partners in operational support, including on the implementation of Security Council resolution 2719 (2023).
29B.84	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Sustainable and multidimensional operational response to escalated situations and unique requirements;

(b)	Fit-for-purpose support solutions that leverage existing capacities and expertise across the Secretariat and with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes to enhance efficiency, responsiveness, interoperability and operational continuity;
(c)	Mutually beneficial collaboration between Secretariat and non-Secretariat entities, including Member States and regional organizations, that addresses priority needs across Secretariat entities;

(d)	Measurable gains in efficiency and responsiveness across the Secretariat operational support architecture, including deepened cooperation in support operations with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes.
Programme performance in 2025
United Nations Support Office in Haiti established

29B.85	In 2025, the Security Council adopted resolution 2793 (2025), by which it requested the Secretary-General to establish the United Nations Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH) to provide support, primarily to the Gang Suppression Force, the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti, the Haitian National Police and the Haitian armed forces, on any joint operations with the Force, as well as technical support to the Organization of American States. Following the adoption of the resolution, the subprogramme immediately initiated and led all action necessary for the establishment of UNSOH. Key achievements, in close collaboration and cooperation with other Secretariat entities, included the preparation and signing of an agreement with the Government of Haiti regarding the grant, occupancy and use of land and premises; the designation of the Dominican Republic as the medical evacuation destination for UNSOH-supported personnel and consent to host an office for non-location-dependent functions to be operationalized on 20 January 2026; the preparation of a template memorandum of understanding concerning reimbursement of eligible categories of contingent-owned equipment in relation to the Gang Suppression Force in Haiti, to be concluded with countries contributing personnel to the Force; the preparation of a draft agreement between the United Nations and the Government concerning the status of UNSOH; and the initiation of procurement action for critical requirements of UNSOH, including civilian aviation capability, medical evacuation services, the provision of rations and fuel, medical services, freight forwarding and the establishment of infrastructure to support the Gang Suppression Force and UNSOH.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened support partnership arrangements – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.92	The Department has put in place a support partnership framework to facilitate an active approach to establishing and drawing on partnership arrangements within the Department and across the Secretariat. As part of this effort, the Department introduced support guidelines in reviewing and managing support partnership arrangements in a more consistent and coordinated manner, helping to clarify roles and streamline internal processes.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.93	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, notwithstanding the establishment of a support partnership framework and related institutional partnership initiatives, gaps remained in translating partnership engagement into consistent practice. While initial scoping and relationship-building with designated focal points has helped to identify opportunities for cooperation with key partners, these efforts have not always progressed to formalized arrangements or sustained implementation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will establish a departmental-wide governance mechanism to provide strategic direction for support partnerships, ensure alignment with operational priorities and support accountability for advancing partnerships from engagement to delivery. This will help to strengthen coherence, clarify roles and ownership and reinforce a more results-oriented approach to support partnerships.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Administration, New York
Objective

29B.96	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure a safe and fit-for-purpose physical environment at Headquarters and responsive facilities, as well as administrative, logistical, information and other services in support of the efficient functioning of the Organization and a public informed of the work of the United Nations.
Strategy
29B.97	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Proactively maintain and operate the Headquarters buildings to achieve efficiencies, promote a modern workplace and preserve the property value of the campus, with an emphasis on reviewing building components completed more than 10 years ago and improving accessibility for persons with disabilities and environmental sustainability;

(b)	Regularly adjust the long-term planning of office accommodation to support the needs of entities in New York, centred on implementing the recommendations from the mandated assessment of the real estate portfolio (see General Assembly resolution 75/253 C);

(c)	Provide efficient, timely and cost-effective services to clients in New York in the areas of building management, office space, asset and official gift management, inventory and warehouse management, travel and transportation, mail and pouch services, event management, postal administration and catering and other commercial operations;

(d)	Provide effective archives and records management services to Secretariat entities globally and strengthen the subprogramme’s capacity to manage the accelerated shift from paper to digital archives;

(e)	Coordinate and manage commercial insurance policies to mitigate and transfer risk pertaining to property, terrorism and liability programmes, as well as the malicious acts insurance policy, for the Organization, including offices away from Headquarters and peacekeeping and special political missions; adjudicate claims emanated from these insurance programmes and obtain settlements from the insurers; and provide insurance advice for vendor, memorandum of understanding and pro bono contracts and agreements;
(f)	Ensure accurate and timely disbursement of salaries and related allowances for all United Nations Secretariat staff;
(g)	Process other disbursements in respect of the financial obligations of the Organization and process the tax reimbursement claims of United States taxpayers.
29B.98	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A highly functional, more inclusive and sustainable campus that symbolizes the ideals of the United Nations;
(b)	High client satisfaction and cost-efficiency in the provision of services;
(c)	Increased public awareness of the past and current work of the Organization.
Programme performance in 2025
Rental cost recovery automated through payroll

29B.99	The rental obligations for leased buildings at Headquarters are funded from the regular budget, the peacekeeping support account and cost recovery for extrabudgetary positions. In previous years, the cost-recovery process required extensive engagement with all entities to review their extrabudgetary-funded positions based on Umoja staffing tables and to determine the total rental charge for each entity, taking into account the projected incumbency of each position for the year. Once an agreement was reached, cost-recovery transactions were processed manually, for each position or through a monthly batch process, depending on the availability of funding. This approach required continuous data reconciliation and the execution of thousands of manual transactions annually. In 2025, the subprogramme introduced a payroll-based cost-recovery mechanism, fully automating the process. This significantly reduced administrative overhead or burden and improved accuracy based on actual incumbency.

children.text: Result 3: increased accessibility of premises and facilities at United Nations Headquarters for persons with disabilities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29B.105	In 2018, the subprogramme commissioned a baseline accessibility assessment of the Headquarters premises. Following the initial assessment, the subprogramme implemented a three-year programme to upgrade the United Nations facilities and premises to meet basic accessibility levels, with a focus on the physical accessibility of United Nations buildings. By the end of 2023, the Department had implemented improvements, including the installation of automatic doors to several conference rooms, a wheelchair accessible exit gate at the staff entrance and wheelchair charging stations, and a lift in the General Assembly rostrum and an accessible ramp at the General Assembly 200 entrance, as well as modifications to a select portion of the floor seating in the General Assembly Hall and the modification of all curb cuts on campus to meet accessibility standards and provide safe, barrier-free access for persons with disabilities.
Lessons learned and planned change

29B.106	The lesson for the subprogramme was that further improvements could be made to enhance the accessibility of premises and facilities at United Nations Headquarters to go beyond meeting the minimum standards. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will implement a five-year programme, which commenced in 2025, to further upgrade the premises and facilities to enhance accessibility for persons with disabilities. Accordingly, the subprogramme completed the first phase of its second accessibility assessment focused on the routes used by delegates, staff and visitors. This assessment has and will continue to inform the implementation of further improvements to the premises and facilities. The second phase of this accessibility assessment is planned for 2027.
